Drivers of Risk: Annual mean temperature, temperature fluctuations, temperature bins, seasonal mean temperature, temperature variation, temperature shocks, global warming (temperature increases), emissions scenarios.
Methodology: The study analyzes year-to-year temperature fluctuations from 1975-2016 in 152 countries, using fixed effects panel regression models with temperature polynomials and interactions with GDP per capita to assess the impact of global warming on obesity. It employs a baseline model and a long-difference model, comparing year-to-year and 30-year temperature trends to infer adaptation effects, while considering various control variables and robustness checks. The study also uses multi-model ensemble climate projections (CMIP6) and country-level climate measures from the LandScan Program, incorporating emissions scenarios and temperature bins to assess impacts from 2016-2050.
Highlights: Global warming primarily increases obesity rates in temperate zones while sometimes reducing them in tropical regions; adaptation may not fully mitigate this impact; the effect varies based on income, age, education, and sex, with poorer countries, older populations, more educated adults, and males being more affected; temperature increases initially raise and then lower obesity, with a threshold around 27°C; higher temperatures may decrease calorie expenditure; long-term adaptation may not significantly reduce the impact of global warming on obesity.
More Notes: Temperature distributions are key to the analysis; adaptation inferences are not solely driven by adaptation; analysis is limited for countries with mean temperatures below zero; income (GDP) moderates the effect of temperature on obesity; heterogeneity of impact is examined concerning income, age, education, gender, and region; the 12-15 °C temperature bin was excluded due to collinearity; seasonal models provide more detail but may miss non-linear effects; temperature variation and shocks are defined using specific equations and thresholds; a flowchart summarizes factors influencing obesity; the response function is normalized for comparison; short-run and long-run marginal effects are distinguished, with maps depicting projected changes; a more active role for the public sector is suggested; limitations include lack of long-term sub-country obesity data and BMI scale data.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is assessed by comparing short-run (year-to-year temperature variations) and long-run (30-year temperature trends) models, inferring adaptation from the differences. A long-difference approach compares 30-year periods. The interaction between income and temperature is investigated. Potential channels (income, food prices, etc.) are examined.

Paper Title: Temperature and GDP: A review of climate econometrics analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Economic impacts of climate change, GDP effects (growth and level), damage modeling.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature, Extreme Temperatures, Temperature Variability, Deviations from Historical Norms, Weather Shocks, Average Climate Conditions, GDP Growth, GDP Level, Productivity, Physical Capital Stock
Methodology: Climate econometrics models relate temperature and GDP using linear and non-linear forms, different temporal resolutions (annual, monthly, seasonal), panel models, and other techniques to distinguish between short- and long-run climate impacts and adaptation effects; Employs temperature bins, cross-sectional models (capturing long-term equilibrium after adaptation), and panel models with fixed effects and time trends; Introduces the long difference approach (averaging variables over time) and hybrid panel models to distinguish short-, medium-, and long-term variations; Uses the half-panel jackknife FE (HPJ-FE) estimator to address feedback effects; Advocates for differentiating between short- and long-run effects to identify adaptation effects, refining long difference models in growth rates, and focusing on deviation from long-run averages in panel data.
Highlights: Growth effects of temperature on GDP are generally larger than level effects. Accurate modeling of adaptation and distinction between short- and long-run effects are crucial. Non-linear models are preferred over linear specifications for long-term climate impact assessment, with temperature bins used to reveal non-linear effects and thresholds. Hybrid panel models capture long-term climate impacts and distinguish short- and long-term effects. Climate econometrics research can provide empirical evidence for improving damage functions in IAMs. Deviation from historical temperature norms is important for avoiding spurious correlations and capturing net impacts after adaptation. Multiple model specifications should be incorporated to distinguish growth and level effects.
More Notes: Review of climate econometrics literature identifies limitations in existing studies and suggests future research directions, including addressing biases from omitted variables and endogenous control variables, identifying key temperature thresholds for adaptation, improving adaptation modeling by addressing data gaps on costs and benefits, and capturing non-market sector impacts beyond GDP. It advocates for multiple model specifications, exploring temperature's effect on GDP through diverse temperature variables, modeling temperature shocks on GDP growth in IAMs, investigating adaptation costs and effects, and examining temperature effects in trading networks.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Adaptation effects are explored by differentiating between short- and long-run climate impacts, employing panel models (with fixed effects and time trends) and long difference approaches. Cross-sectional models are used to capture long-term equilibrium effects after adaptation. Hybrid panel models and the HPJ-FE estimator are used to address feedback effects. Refined long difference models and analyses of deviations from long-run averages are suggested to improve the modeling of adaptation.

Paper Title: Advancing Weather and Climate Forecasting for Our Changing World
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced detrimental societal and economic impact of hazardous weather and climate events, especially for low-income nations.
Drivers of Risk: Extreme weather events, warming climate, meteorological/hydrological/climatological hazards, population growth, urbanization, coastal populations, changing global economy, insufficient access to forecasts in low-income countries, disproportionate impact on poor nations.
Methodology: Methodology involves summarizing expert views, analyzing NatCatSERVICE data (Munich Re, 2019), reviewing advancements in NWP models and collaborative efforts (WMO's WWW & GDPFS), and proposing improved predictive skill through HPC, observations, advanced models, ensemble forecasting, AI/ML (TensorFlow, PyTorch, Python, C++) for data assimilation, parameterization, and downscaling, emphasizing open data sharing, reforecasts, training, and public-private-academic partnerships.
Highlights: Extreme weather events are increasing, disproportionately impacting low-income nations; advances in Earth system modeling, data sharing (though access to regional and private sector data remains a challenge), and AI can improve forecasting skill and utilization of observing systems; improved coordination among public, private, and academic sectors and investment in low-income countries are needed to address big data challenges and support sustainable development through improved weather and climate services.
More Notes: Climate change is increasing extreme weather events globally, particularly in North America and Europe, but also increasingly in South America, Africa, and Australia; advancing weather and climate forecasting requires a collaborative innovation cycle involving public, private, and academic sectors, focusing on infrastructure, R&D, and operations, leveraging technology, international collaboration, and addressing numerical weather prediction challenges; improved forecasting in low-income countries requires capacity building and technology transfer.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through improving predictive skill using high-performance computing, better observational instrumentation, advanced modeling, and better model initialization. AI/ML techniques can downscale forecast data and cloud-based NWP systems can be leveraged, enabled by partnerships between private, public, and academic sectors. Investment in expertise and infrastructure, data sharing, and integrating multimodels are also noted, with emphasis on strategic alignment and regional cooperation with low-income countries.

Paper Title: Global warming and heat extremes to enhance inflationary pressures
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased food and headline inflation globally, particularly at low latitudes and during summer at high latitudes.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature (average, variability, extremes), precipitation (extremes, SPEI), emission scenarios (SSP126, SSP585), seasonality, regionality.
Methodology: Fixed-effects panel regressions are applied to monthly price indices from 121 countries (1996-2021) combined with weather data (ERA5, 1990-2021, and CRU TS v4.05, 1901-2021 for SPEI); the analysis uses over 27,000 observations and examines temperature shocks relative to 30-year moving averages, with robustness tests employing dynamic panel specifications, Driscoll-Kraay errors, monetary policy controls, and alternative inflation databases; the empirical models are then used with CMIP6 climate model projections (SSP585 and SSP126) to estimate the impacts of future weather conditions on inflation, focusing on 2035 and 2060, considering adaptation, policy response, and macroeconomic interactions, using population weighting and trend-preserving statistical downscaling for historical temperatures.
Highlights: Climate change, particularly higher temperatures, leads to persistent (over 12 months) increases in both food and headline inflation, impacting both high- and low-income countries. These inflationary pressures are non-linear, exacerbated by increased daily temperature variability, and largest at low latitudes with strong seasonal variations (peaking in summer) at high latitudes. Projections for 2035 suggest significant upward pressure on inflation, with extreme heat events already impacting European inflation and projected to worsen. Climate change will likely alter inflation seasonality, increase its volatility and heterogeneity, and create persistent upward pressure, with stronger impacts in Northern mid-to-high latitudes in summer.
More Notes: Climate impacts on inflation vary by season and region based on climatic norms, with average temperature changes having the strongest effect; the study uses SPEI to account for wet/dry conditions. Inflation pressures are seasonally heterogeneous, potentially altering the food inflation cycle. Detecting precipitation impacts is limited by data and spatial variability. The study acknowledges adaptation's potential to limit inflation, but emphasizes the need for emissions mitigation and notes limits to historical adaptation. Analysis uses gridded population estimates to account for precipitation's spatial variability and focuses on deviations from historical seasonal patterns (1990-2021), assuming adaptation to these patterns. The study focuses on the impacts of deviations from normal seasonal conditions, but doesn't control for employment or economic output. The inflation impacts are evaluations, not projections, and don't account for changing temperature variability affecting extremes, while holding demographic developments, consumption, and fiscal/monetary policies constant.
Adaptation considered: Generally abstracted from. Explicitly states results are in the absence of unprecedented adaptation and abstracts from adaptation and policy response. Some modeling of structural changes in the economy is included.

Paper Title: Climate, Environment and Socio-Economic Drivers of Global Agricultural Productivity Growth
Outcome(s) of Interest: Growth in global agricultural TFP, climate impacts, productivity changes, technological progress, climate variability effects.
Drivers of Risk: Climate variability (temperature & rainfall), long-term temperature (LTT), long-term precipitation (LTP), weather regimes (arid, tropical, temperate), land elevation.
Methodology: A panel data analysis (1969-2013) of 104 countries decomposes agricultural production growth (Total Factor Productivity - TFP) into technology, efficiency, and scale changes. It employs a counterfactual model and MVTOBIT model to assess climate change, natural production environment, and socio-economic factors' influence on TFP. Climate variables include long-term (30-year moving average) precipitation and temperature, and annual temperature variability (difference between monthly max/min average temperature). Regression equations are used to calculate marginal effects of climate variables on TFP and efficiency. TFP is predicted to 2033 under four climatic scenarios based on historical data. A one-way ANOVA test is used to determine if TFP change indices across categories are significantly different and econometric analysis examines the impacts of crop diversification on TFP.
Highlights: Global agricultural TFP has grown consistently at 0.44% annually, driven by technology and mix-efficiency changes, with climate variability negatively impacting efficiency and yield. Crop diversification, country-specific R&D, policies, crop insurance, and technology adoption can enhance resilience and mitigate climate impacts.
More Notes: The study, published in *Land* under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license, examines agricultural TFP growth using data from sources like IFPRI, World Bank, FAOSTAT, and EM-DAT; it includes regional dummies for Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Latin America and Caribbean, East Asia and the Pacific, and Europe and Central Asia, and considers World Bank income classifications; findings indicate significant growth in technical, scale, and mix efficiency over time.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly mentioned in the provided text. The study focuses on identifying climate change impacts on agricultural productivity and predicting future impacts under different climate scenarios, while accounting for technology, socio-economic factors, and agro-ecological factors. The analysis models TFP changes based on historical data and parameter estimates, but does not directly address adaptive strategies or behaviors.

Paper Title: Addition of Biochar to Green Roof Substrate to Enhance Plant Performance: A Long-Term Field Study
Outcome(s) of Interest: Enhanced plant growth; reduced building energy costs; shorter payback; reduced structural requirements.
Drivers of Risk: Substrate temperature, weather conditions, solar radiation, winds, moisture/nutrient availability, biochar amendment, water retention, drought stress, ambient temperature fluctuations, substrate thickness, maintenance, nutrient-deficient substrates.
Methodology: Six 1m² green roof test beds were constructed on a building rooftop in Australia (temperate oceanic climate) to investigate the impact of biochar amendment rate (0%, 7.5%, 15%), particle size (fine, medium), and application methods (mixing, top-dressing, bottom-applied) on the performance of wallaby grass, common everlasting, and billy buttons from May 5, 2023, to May 16, 2024; Data collected included plant height (fortnightly), coverage area (end of study), dry weight (end of study, after drying at 60°C for 48 h), and substrate temperature (continuously at 5 min intervals at 10 cm depth); Data analyzed using observational data and CHEMFLO-2000 modeling.
Highlights: Biochar improves green roof plant growth, particularly with medium-sized particles at a 15% amendment rate, leading to reduced substrate temperature and building energy consumption; this is achieved through increased water retention and nutrient availability, although effectiveness varies based on application method, plant species, and climate.
More Notes: Green roof research assesses biochar's impact (rates, methods, sizes) on plant performance (Wallaby Grass, Common Everlasting, Billy Buttons), linking to urban heat island mitigation; integrates GRs with PV for cooling and electricity boost; tracks weather, notes fertilizer use; identifies medium biochar particles (1-3mm) as slightly better than fine; calls for multi-year observations, temperature/moisture data at different substrate depths, and investigation of application methods for optimal plant/substrate combinations under varying climate conditions.
Adaptation considered: Biochar amended green roofs are investigated as a potential adaptation strategy to improve plant performance under changing climate conditions. The study explores different biochar amendment rates, application methods, and particle sizes within green roof systems. Measured plant performance metrics include height, coverage, and dry weight and substrate temperature is monitored. This aims to provide insight into optimizing green roof design for enhanced resilience.

Paper Title: Electric Vehicle Adoption: A Comprehensive Systematic Review of Technological, Environmental, Organizational and Policy Impacts
Outcome(s) of Interest: Substantial reductions in greenhouse gas emissions; lifecycle emissions reductions compared to ICEVs; operational efficiency and sustainability for businesses.
Drivers of Risk: GHG emissions from ICEVs; resource-intensive battery production; electricity grid composition; vehicle usage patterns; energy consumption/CO2 emissions in recycling/charging infrastructure; material choices; tire production/wear; battery degradation; environmental conditions (cold climates, weather, shading/cloud cover); lithium-ion battery disposal; power generation mix (LCCO2); policy evolution; sustainability practices; technological advancements.
Methodology: Systematic review of 88 peer-reviewed articles (2019-2024) from Scopus using PRISMA 2020 guidelines; bibliometric analysis with VOSviewer for keyword co-occurrence mapping; thematic content analysis; lifecycle assessment (LCA) for environmental performance; considered simulation, case studies, input-output analysis, and scenario analysis; focused on journal articles in English; risk of bias mitigated through selection of studies with robust methodologies.
Highlights: EV adoption significantly reduces GHG emissions and promotes environmental sustainability, particularly when coupled with grid decarbonization and renewable energy sources; lifecycle assessments consistently show lower emissions for BEVs compared to ICEVs, despite higher manufacturing emissions; adoption leads to energy and cost savings, job creation, and improved air quality, requiring strategic planning, infrastructure investment, and innovative technologies like advanced batteries, smart charging, and efficient recycling methods to maximize benefits and overcome challenges related to grid stability and resource management; international collaboration, especially with the US and China, is crucial for advancing EV technology and policy.
More Notes: EV adoption research encompasses technological advancements (battery tech, energy storage), environmental impacts (GHG emissions reduction, lifecycle assessments), organizational changes (fleet management), and policy implications (incentives, infrastructure). Key direct climate impacts include reduced emissions from EVs compared to ICEVs (especially with renewable energy), the carbon footprint of battery production and charging infrastructure (DC fast chargers), the importance of cleaner electricity grids and battery tech advancements, tire production and use, and the need for efficient battery recycling methods and a circular economy, supported by government policies and optimized charging strategies. Grid stability, efficient energy management, and technology adoption challenges in diverse climates are also relevant.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is examined through a systematic review of 88 peer-reviewed articles, with methodologies including bibliometric and thematic content analysis. The review focuses on electric vehicle integration, sustainability, lifecycle assessments, and optimization strategies. Emphasis is placed on linking themes like EV adoption and climate goals, including emissions reductions and lifecycle CO2 emission comparisons, revealing the extent of adaptation considerations in these domains.

Paper Title: Integrating deep learning, satellite image processing, and spatial-temporal analysis for urban flood prediction
Outcome(s) of Interest: Rapid, accurate flood extent prediction; continuous flood monitoring; infrastructure impact assessment; early warning systems; insurance risk assessment.
Drivers of Risk: Urban development (impervious surfaces), climate change (extreme rainfall).
Methodology: A U-Net CNN model predicts near-real time urban flood extents using Sentinel-1 imagery, terrain data, and daily rainfall; model training/testing split: 70%/30%.
Highlights: Developed a deep learning model for near real-time urban flood mapping with a Ground Truth Index of 84.05%, achieving 10-meter resolution and extending application beyond traditional flood mapping to enable more comprehensive monitoring.
More Notes: Sensitivity analysis reveals the relative importance of different input data combinations in the flood prediction model.
Adaptation considered: Model predicts flood extents from rainfall, incorporating terrain and satellite data, enabling near-real-time awareness.

Paper Title: Re-examining the impact of annual weather fluctuations on global livestock production
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decrease in global beef production; amplified effects in specific regions; precipitation effects vary by climate zone; projected future output decline.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature increase, precipitation changes (increase/decrease, droughts, floods), pathogen/parasite activity.
Methodology: Country-level panel data analysis (1961-2017) using an established econometric strategy, including country fixed effects and region-specific trends; analyzes national livestock production and local weather fluctuations from 187 countries; uses cattle production data from FAOSTAT and historical weather data from the University of Delaware; employs cattle population-weighted weather averages; adjusts standard errors for spatial (1,000km) and serial (3-years) correlation using the approach described in Hsiang (2010); performs Panel Granger causality Wald test; models changes in temperature and precipitation impact on log animal production, uses error-corrected ACCESS SSP3.70, and Long difference estimations, to exploit the exogeneity of cross-time variations in weather, assumes no adaptation other than observed historically.
Highlights: Temperature increases, particularly in hot, poor, and agriculture-dependent countries, significantly reduce global beef production; precipitation's impact varies by climate zone, being positive in tropical regions and negative in temperate ones; forecasts predict substantial declines in animal output due to climate change, with tropical countries being most severely affected, and temperature plays a more significant role than changes in rainfall; findings are robust across various tests, with non-linear relationships between temperature and beef production observed.
More Notes: Beef production is highly sensitive to temperature, with a 1°C increase leading to a 9.7% reduction, amplified in hot, poor, and agriculture-dependent countries; increased precipitation benefits tropical but harms temperate regions; climate change projections indicate a 20% reduction by mid-century and 40% by end-of-century (ACCESS SSP3.70), assuming limited adaptation; these impacts are mediated through cereal yields and production, particularly in hot countries; the model doesn't capture adaptation to gradual changes or inter-annual tradeoffs.
Adaptation considered: The study assumes limited adaptation over the historical period, implicitly excluding future adaptive measures. Adaptation is not explicitly modeled, with analyses relying on historical data without projecting future adaptive responses. The focus is on assessing the direct impacts of weather on livestock production, without accommodating advanced adaptation strategies beyond what has been historically observed.

Paper Title: A Global Increase in Nearshore Tropical Cyclone Intensification
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased nearshore TC intensification, coastal socio-economic threat, climate change impact, vertical wind shear decrease, relative humidity increase
Drivers of Risk: Vertical wind shear, relative humidity, upper troposphere warming, diabatic heating, sea surface temperature, anthropogenic warming, nearshore intensification, SSP585 emissions scenario.
Methodology: The study employs multiple methodologies: analysis of observed TC track data from IBTrACS and ADT-HURSAT; atmospheric reanalyses from NCEP-DOE II and ERA5; high-resolution climate model simulations from HighResMIP and CMIP6, focusing on SSP585; and idealized numerical experiments using a Stationary Wave Model (SWM) with R30 resolution and 24 vertical levels. TC intensification rates are calculated via linear regression of storm maximum wind speed over successive 6-hr intervals, and statistical significance is assessed using Student’s t-tests with Monte Carlo methods for error estimation. Trends in SST, VWS, and RH are examined, with data sub-sampled to ensure statistically similar storm states. Nearshore regions are defined using a ~3° distance threshold. The SWM is used to decompose forcing mechanism effects by comparing CTRL (2015-2034), Future (2081-2100), and sensitivity experiments.
Highlights: Nearshore tropical cyclone intensification rates increased during 1979-2020, particularly near the US, East Asian, and South Asian coasts, due to weakening vertical wind shear and increased relative humidity. Climate models project this intensification to continue, with decreasing wind shear playing a key role, attributed to amplified upper-tropospheric warming and altered heating patterns, although models may underestimate intensity changes. The most significant intensification occurs close to coastlines and implies potentially stronger landfalling storms.
More Notes: Focuses on nearshore TC intensification (within 3° of coastlines, particularly around Central/South America, West/Southern Africa, Northern Australia/Southeast Asia, and islands in the Indian/Western Pacific Oceans), its historical trends (1979-2020), and future projections (SSP585); ERA5 reanalysis supports findings; HighResMIP underestimates intensification; CMIP6 and SWM discrepancies exist potentially due to coupling and parameterization issues; Vertical wind shear (VWS) changes are complex and regionally variable, influenced by SST warming and circulation responses.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this paper. The focus is on analyzing past trends and projecting future changes in tropical cyclone intensification rates using climate models and observational data.

Paper Title: Increase of Simultaneous Soybean Failures Due To Climate Change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased frequency and magnitude of simultaneous extreme soybean crop failures in the Americas under future climate scenarios, potentially mitigated by adaptation to long-term climate trends.
Drivers of Risk: Climate extremes (heatwaves, droughts, hot-dry compounds), temperature changes, precipitation changes, climate variability, mean climate trends, weather-induced crop failures, GCM sensitivity, deforestation, rainfed harvest areas.
Methodology: A hybrid model, coupling a process-based crop model (EPIC-IIASA) with a machine learning model (multilayer perceptron), is developed to improve soybean yield simulation in Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay. The hybrid model uses simulated climate data, simulated yields (GGCM EPIC-IIASA), and observed yields (USDA, IBGE, MAGYP) from 1979-2015, along with climate indices generated using Climpact, to project future soybean yields, fixing harvest areas to 2012 values, and analyzes "impact analogs" (years with larger negative yield anomalies than 2012) under different climate scenarios (SSPs 1-2.6 and 5-8.5) using GGCMs (GFDL-esm4, IPSL-cm6a-lr, UKESM1-0-ll).
Highlights: Long-term climate trends increase the frequency and magnitude of simultaneous soybean failures across the Americas, while changes in climate variability increase failures at the country level but not simultaneously; adaptation to long-term mean climate changes can reduce the risk of simultaneous failures, but without adaptation, 2012-like extreme events become more frequent, especially under high-forcing scenarios; such events are primarily driven by compounding hot and dry conditions; country-level failures are projected to increase, with the US showing the highest number.
More Notes: Soybean production risk analysis focuses on the US, Brazil, and Argentina, utilizing a hybrid crop-machine learning model to assess the impact of climate change (including CO2 fertilization effects) on simultaneous extreme crop failures similar to the 2012 event, distinguishing between risks due to changes in mean climate versus climate variability and emphasizing the importance of joint analysis of yield anomalies across regions, with adaptation to long-term mean trends potentially reducing future risk, though trend removal also removes CO2 fertilization effects, and a reference list includes relevant studies on climate change impacts on agriculture across various scales and aspects.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation scenarios are considered by simulating both adaptation and no adaptation conditions with a hybrid crop model. These analyses use a combination of historical data, modeled climate indices, and yield projections to assess the potential for future soybean failure events similar or larger than the 2012 drought. The approach evaluates climatic conditions, spatial distributions, and future emission scenarios to inform adaptation strategies by comparing baseline and adapted scenarios, thus providing insights into reducing vulnerabilities to extreme climate events. Such models are fine-tuned to accurately capture climate impacts under varying scenarios.

Paper Title: Climate nonlinearities: selection, uncertainty, projections, and damages
Outcome(s) of Interest: Structural uncertainty dominates temperature projections and economic damage estimates.
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic emissions, radiative forcing (greenhouse gases, aerosols, land use change), shared socioeconomic pathways (SSP4-6.0, SSP3-7.0), temperature anomalies, economic damages.
Methodology: A Bayesian approach is employed to quantify structural uncertainty in climate projections using energy-balance models (EBMs). A model selection parameter governs the inclusion of various climate dynamics formulations (linear, T/F-dependent heat capacity and feedback). The methodology involves specifying a model (linear EBM), priors, sampling, likelihood computation, weighting, and probabilistic future projections using radiative forcing timeseries (1850-2020) and future emission scenarios. Damage calculations follow the approach in [10], incorporating Weitzman's [25] damage function and a 4.255% discount rate, also analyzes the impact of structural uncertainty on economic damages and uses publicly available data and code.
Highlights: Structural uncertainty in climate models, particularly concerning temperature-dependent feedbacks, is substantial and dominates overall non-socioeconomic uncertainty in economic projections of climate change damages, impacting both temperature projections and economic damage assessments; reported uncertainties may be underestimated, requiring careful consideration of structural uncertainty in physical and socioeconomic projections using reduced complexity climate models.
More Notes: Structural uncertainty in climate models, particularly in Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs), is significant. A temperature-dependent climate feedback model aligns best with observations, though the sign of temperature dependence differs from complex Earth system models, likely due to simplified representation of the pattern effect. Alternative model structures hold substantial posterior probability, emphasizing the importance of structural uncertainty. Reducing this uncertainty may involve explicitly incorporating phenomena like ENSO or the pattern effect.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in the provided notes. The focus is on quantifying structural uncertainty in climate projections, modeling climate dynamics, projecting future temperature changes, and calculating economic damages using different damage functions and discount rates.

Paper Title: Enhancing the resilience of blue foods to climate shocks using insurance
Outcome(s) of Interest: Economic resilience, sustainable resource use, coastal community adaptation.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; marine heatwaves; harmful algal blooms; hypoxia/low oxygen events; coral bleaching; storms/hurricanes/cyclones; species range shifts; changes in ocean properties (temperature, nutrient availability); El Niño; disease outbreaks; non-linear food web dynamics; extreme weather events.
Methodology: Explores insurance and reinsurance mechanisms (including parametric), moral hazard, and adverse selection in fisheries risk management; addresses risk pricing challenges (limited observations, complex teleconnections); discusses indemnity and parametric insurance, cooperative/mutual funds; proposes a multi-stage implementation process (design, demonstration, capital pool); and suggests an insurance-based risk reduction approach, drawing from agriculture.
Highlights: Financial risk management tools, especially insurance (including ocean-index and parametric types), are crucial for building resilience in blue food supply chains and coastal communities against climate shocks by transferring risk, conserving capital, and incentivizing sustainable practices; however, challenges like data scarcity, moral hazard, adverse selection, attributing impacts, and designing policies sensitive to diverse coastal income landscapes need to be addressed, potentially requiring government subsidies, non-traditional support, and risk adjustments for long-term climate change; government disaster relief is currently inadequate and unsustainable.
More Notes: Collaboration between insurance, marine sectors, government, academia, and NGOs is vital for equitable resilience against climate shocks impacting blue food economies; marine insurance offers an alternative to federal disaster relief, though challenges exist for common pool resources like wild fisheries; improving ocean forecasts enhance insurability; protecting the base of blue food supply chains is crucial; insurance should incentivize adaptation, not prop up failing industries; reinsurance and parametric insurance can act as disaster relief; tying financial tools to regions/groups, robust data, and inclusive engagement are key.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Insurance mechanisms, including parametric insurance, are explored as an adaptation strategy for marine resource users and coastal communities to manage and transfer risk from climate shocks. The potential role of government policies in conjunction with insurance is noted, along with challenges in risk pricing and implementation. The text suggests a multi-stage implementation process and draws parallels with adaptation strategies in agriculture.

Paper Title: How many people will live in poverty because of climate change? A macro-level projection analysis to 2070
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate change increases extreme poverty despite overall poverty decline; 25-78 million additional people in extreme poverty by 2030-2050; Climatic tipping points worsen impacts; Reduced income inequality mitigates effects.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change (temperature, precipitation, tipping points), RCP scenarios (emissions), socioeconomic development (income inequality, economic production, fossil fuel use), depressed agricultural yields, increased prices (food, housing, healthcare), reduced labor productivity.
Methodology: The study projects poverty across income thresholds for 175 countries through 2070 using the International Futures (IFs) model, a six-sector general equilibrium model, to simulate economic production, consumption, and poverty rates under different climate scenarios. It combines Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) with economic growth and income distribution series from the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) to model economic production, income distribution, and population change. The model incorporates damage functions, including the DICE model damage function, to gauge economic losses from worsening climate conditions and operationalizes the impact of tipping points using findings from Dietz et al. (2021). Sensitivity analysis was performed by exogenously changing the Gini coefficient for income inequality. Replication instructions for the IFs model are available online.
Highlights: Climate change significantly impacts poverty by affecting prices, productivity, and income, hindering socioeconomic development. Climate change effects are modeled to impact productivity and increase poverty, driven by rising temperatures, precipitation anomalies, and climate tipping points, with agricultural activity identified as an important channel. Climate change is projected to reduce GDP and increase global inequality. The impact of climate change on poverty can be mitigated with sustained efforts to improve income distribution. Tipping points pose a substantial threat to future socioeconomic development and increase poverty.
More Notes: The paper addresses the tension between economic development, climate change, and poverty, considering the impacts of income inequality and climate tipping points while acknowledging the energy requirements of human development and its environmental consequences.
Adaptation considered: No explicit adaptation measures are mentioned. The analysis focuses on modeling climate change impacts on poverty using SSPs, RCPs, the IFs model, damage functions, and tipping points. Sensitivity analysis of income inequality is performed.

Paper Title: Near-term transition and longer-term physical climate risks of greenhouse gas emissions pathways
Outcome(s) of Interest: Physical risks (heatwaves, drought, heat stress, crop impacts) by 2050; Transition risks (mitigation costs, carbon prices, fossil fuel demand, coal plant reductions) by 2030; Balance between transition costs and physical threats.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature pathways, GHG emissions, socio-economics, technology, policy, heatwaves, drought, flooding, crop changes, fossil fuel demand, carbon pricing, abatement costs, regional coordination.
Methodology: Integrated framework uses GCAM (v5.1, technology-rich, regionally disaggregated IAM) coupled with MAGICC4.2 (reduced complexity climate model) and spatially-explicit global-scale impacts models to explore transition (to 2030) and physical (to 2050) risks under various emissions/temperature pathways (1.5-4°C), considering policy, technology, and socioeconomic factors (including SSP1 & SSP3); calculates regional hazard and impact attributes using seven physical hazard indicators, normalized to 2050 (physical) and 2030 (transition) values with the 2°C Central scenario using a 1981-2010 reference period.
Highlights: Highlights: Physical risks by 2050 depend on the temperature pathway, while transition risks by 2030 are sensitive to temperature pathways and influenced by technological, policy, and socio-economic factors. An integrated framework assesses physical and transition risks globally and in key regions (China, USA, EU+UK, India). Major heatwaves and agricultural drought have non-linear relationships with temperature, leading to significant differences between scenarios. India is vulnerable to river flooding and agricultural drought under the NDC Pledges scenario. Stringent mitigation leads to higher abatement costs but lower physical climate hazards; similar long-term physical risks can have different near-term transition risks. The analysis explores impacts under various scenarios, allowing comparison of mitigation pathways and focusing on physical and transition risks, drawing from indicators that track progress towards the Paris Agreement goals. Disorderly scenarios and different technology pathways are explored, including scenarios that miss the 2°C target and a 1.5°C scenario emphasizing rapid decarbonization and end-use electrification.
More Notes: The study explores climate risks (both physical and transition) under various warming scenarios (1.5-4°C) using 11 scenarios for a 2°C target, highlighting the interplay between socio-economic factors, technology (renewables, nuclear, CCS), and policy choices. It acknowledges uncertainty, uses median estimates, and compares risk ratios to a 2°C Central scenario. Transition risks are sensitive to temperature pathways (mitigation costs, carbon prices), while physical risks (heatwaves, drought, heat stress) depend greatly on the temperature pathway by 2050. The research emphasizes the lack of a single low-carbon transition pathway and the need for futures analysis beyond systems modeling, including considering extremes. It uses GCAM for emissions modeling but separate modules for climate impacts, factoring in land-use change emissions and distinguishing between water-limited and temperature-limited regions. The analysis complements NGFS scenarios but doesn't convert metrics into value-at-risk or consider the stimulus effect of low-carbon investment. Funding came from ClimateWorks Foundation and the H2020 PARIS REINFORCE project.
Adaptation considered: Consideration of adaptation appears limited, primarily focusing on mitigation through different emissions scenarios and their impacts. Socio-economic scenarios (SSP1, SSP3, five SSPs with damage functions) are explored, acknowledging that context matters. Page 16 mentions the importance of considering adaptation measures but does not elaborate on how they are specifically modeled or integrated. The analysis focuses on physical hazards and impacts under different warming levels, not explicitly modeling adaptive responses.

Paper Title: Capturing uncertainty when modelling environmental drivers of fish populations, with an illustrative application to Pacific Cod in the eastern Bering Sea
Outcome(s) of Interest: Negative impacts of climate change on Pacific cod outweigh positive; current harvest rules may lead to stock collapse; pessimistic climate scenarios offer better long-term conservation and profits.
Drivers of Risk: Environmental variables, temperature, ocean pH, sea surface temperature (SST), climate scenarios, Earth System Models, emission scenarios, recruitment, growth, Pacific cod, sea ice, metabolic demand, mortality, trophic interactions, ecosystem impacts, population dynamics.
Methodology: The methodology projects future catch series for Eastern Bering Sea Pacific cod, accounting for uncertainty in model structure, environmental drivers, and Earth System Model/emission scenarios (CMIP6, SSP126 & SSP585). It uses a stock assessment model with age and length classes, incorporating environmental variables (sea surface temperature) to influence length at age-1.5 and mean recruitment. Bayesian methods with NUTS MCMC are used for parameter estimation and to sample catch series from posterior distributions. Projections to 2100 utilize dynamically downscaled hindcasts/projections from Earth System Models (GFDL-ESM4, CESM-CAM6, MIROC-ES2L) with bias correction. The analysis combines four assessment model configurations with six environmental scenarios and two harvest control rules (40% control rule and constant F control rule), considering structural, process, and parameter uncertainty. Relationships between population parameters and environmental variables are selected using model selection approaches, assuming stationarity in these relationships and no stock-recruitment relationship.
Highlights: Including environmental drivers in stock assessments is crucial, but often neglects multiple uncertainties. Higher temperatures increase Pacific cod weight-at-age but reduce recruitment, with the latter effect dominating, potentially leading to stock decline under current harvest control rules. Accounting for structural model uncertainty, process error, and parameter uncertainty is essential. Basing future catches on pessimistic environmental scenarios (GFDL ssp585) may better conserve the stock and achieve long-term profits. Assessment model configuration is also significant, and incorporating a stock-recruitment relationship is important for future work. Earth System Model uncertainty must be considered, as differences in projected biomass and catch exist even under the same emission scenario, especially long-term. Updating projections and reviewing environmental variable relationships is needed. Climate effects from high warming rates can negate harvest control rule benefits by the end of the century.
More Notes: The paper presents a framework for climate-enhanced single-species model projections incorporating uncertainties from model selection, environmental drivers, Earth System Models, emission scenarios, parameter uncertainty, and process error. It covers historical (1970-2022) and projected (2022-2100) conditions, highlighting the low explained variance for environmental drivers and comparing catch limit strategies under climate change. The study emphasizes model uncertainty and trade-offs, acknowledging limitations in environmental variable projections due to data constraints and recommending further exploration of biological processes mediating temperature effects on Pacific cod.
Adaptation considered: Catch projections are made under different climate scenarios (CMIP6, SSPs) and harvest control rules. Model configurations and selection relate environmental variables to recruitment and biomass, but relationships are assumed to be stationary. The impact of different harvest control rules are tested.

Paper Title: The impact of fossil fuel combustion on children's health and the associated losses of human capital
Outcome(s) of Interest: Children's health deterioration, impaired human capital in adulthood, reduced economic potential.
Drivers of Risk: Fossil fuel combustion, climate change, air pollution, early-life exposure, high temperatures, air pollutants, undernutrition, infections, adverse birth outcomes.
Methodology: Literature review and meta-analysis of existing research are used to assess the impact of climate change and air pollution on children's health, cognitive, and non-cognitive skills.
Highlights: Climate change and air pollution harm children's health, negatively impacting cognitive and non-cognitive development, potentially leading to reduced adult earnings and economic productivity; pollution-induced premature birth/low birth weight could also lead to specific behavioral tendencies.
More Notes: A holistic assessment of climate change and air pollution's impacts on children is needed, considering direct health damages, long-term human capital losses, and associated economic costs, necessitating cross-disciplinary collaboration.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this paper, which focuses on the health and cognitive impacts of fossil fuel-driven air pollution and climate change on children.

Paper Title: Changes in Extreme Daily Precipitation over the Contiguous United States from Convection-Permitting Simulations
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased extreme precipitation in Midwest/Ohio Valley (Dec-May), decreased in southern Great Plains (Dec-Feb), increased variability and return period precipitation amounts across CONUS.
Drivers of Risk: Extreme daily precipitation; Anthropogenic climate change; 99th percentile precipitation; Regional hydroclimate; Water scarcity; Tropical cyclone precipitation; Return period precipitation amounts; Spatiotemporal patterns of heavy precipitation.
Methodology: Dynamically downscaled, convection-permitting WRF-ARW (v4.1.2, 3.75 km grid) simulation, forced by bias-corrected CESM GCM output, was used to model precipitation for historical (HIST, 1990-2005) and future (EOC, 2085-2100) epochs under RCP4.5 (EOC4.5) and RCP8.5 (EOC8.5) scenarios; daily precipitation was derived from AFWA data; HIST was compared to PRISM data for validation; extreme value analysis (EVA) using Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko theorem with block maxima (BM, 1-year block size, Gumbel distribution selected by AIC and log-likelihood) and peak over threshold (POT) methods was implemented; statistical significance was determined using a Mann-Whitney U test (p < 0.05, field significance false discovery rate of 0.1) and 1000-iteration bootstrap sampling for return periods; model performance was evaluated using metrics like r, p, RMSE, and R2, with Q-Q plots for visual assessment, focusing on eight cities and analyzing changes in the 99th percentile of daily precipitation seasonally.
Highlights: Projected increases in extreme daily precipitation in the Cascade Mountains, Ohio River Valley, Northeast, Midwest, Southeast, and Pacific Northwest by the end of the 21st century, with variability in precipitation extremes increasing in eight climatologically unique CONUS cities and significant increases in return period precipitation amounts for most cities; Decreases are projected for the Southwest and southern Great Plains, particularly in the Intermountain West under the EOC8.5 scenario with sporadic decreases in the Southwest, northern Great Plains, and Southeast for June-August in EOC8.5; the model tends to underestimate the highest extreme values.
More Notes: The study investigates extreme daily precipitation across the CONUS by analyzing eight distinct cities and comparing historical data (HIST) with future scenarios (EOC4.5 and EOC8.5). It highlights statistically significant wet and dry biases in the historical model, changes in extreme precipitation patterns, and potential infrastructure impacts due to increased return period precipitation values. It underscores the need for comprehensive future studies focusing on subdaily precipitation extremes and larger ensemble approaches while noting significant increases in precipitation return periods and their socioeconomic implications, particularly for flood risk management.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not directly considered. The study focuses on modeling and analyzing future extreme precipitation events under different climate scenarios, without explicitly addressing adaptive measures.

Paper Title: Housing market dynamics of the post-Sandy Hudson estuary, Long Island Sound, and New Jersey coastline are explained by NFIP participation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Long-term decline in home values, particularly in tracts with fluctuating NFIP participation.
Drivers of Risk: Flooding (past events, Hurricane Sandy, surge & rainfall), flood insurance (NFIP participation), community risk perception (awareness, anxiousness, preparedness).
Methodology: The study employs a socio-environmental (SE) model to analyze the temporal impacts of flooding on mean housing values (HPs) across 496 coastal census tracts from 1970-2021. The SE model simulates annual economic losses, population density, community flood awareness, and preparedness, driven by a time series of peak annual flood hazards and calibrated to observed NFIP claims, policies, population density, and mean HPs. The model incorporates time-varying HP models, including sigmoid, Interrupted Time Series (ITS), and K-shaped recovery models, to simulate housing price trends; the ITS recovery model was found to best capture flooding dynamics in the study region. Statistical correlation analysis was performed between SE model parameters related to flood awareness, preparedness, housing prices, and tract-scale behaviors and perceptions.
Highlights: Hurricane Sandy's initial economic impact was largely absorbed by NFIP, but the region experienced a long-term decline in home values; this decline is linked to community-scale risk perceptions and NFIP participation, with increased NFIP uptake after floods correlating with larger home value decreases; housing values are driven more by perceptions and NFIP participation than actual flood risk, and models show trade-offs between simulating housing prices and losses accurately.
More Notes: Study investigates long-term impacts of Hurricane Sandy on home values (HPs) in coastal NY, CT, and NJ using a socio-environmental model, finding that past flooding salience and NFIP participation are important HP drivers, with increased post-flood insurance changes correlating with larger HP decreases; the ITS model accurately represented flooding dynamics; HP drop and recovery are controlled by parameters b2 and b3, respectively; model code available online.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: The reviewed paper incorporates adaptation through socio-environmental models that emphasize community perceptions, awareness, and preparedness in response to flooding impacts. These models simulate the dynamic relationship between flood hazards, economic losses, and housing prices, taking into account community risk perception and behavior over time. Various recovery models (sigmoid, ITS, K-shaped) capture different adaptation responses, with the ITS model being most effective for reflecting regional flooding dynamics. The models also consider temporal changes in housing prices and their feedback on future flood loss estimations, leveraging socioeconomic data for scenario simulations and calibration.

Paper Title: Creeping disaster along the US coastline: Understanding exposure to sea level rise and hurricanes through historical development
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased property at risk, uneven distribution of losses, faster development in SLR zones, convergence of density in less-developed coastal areas, saturation effects in early settled SLR zones, growing importance of adaptation policies for existing buildings.
Drivers of Risk: Sea level rise (SLR), hurricanes, exposure, coastal development, urbanization.
Methodology: The study examines U.S. coastal urban development's exposure to sea level rise using historical settlement data from HISDAC-US and ZTRAX, covering 1810-2015. It employs gridded data analysis, defining SLR zones via NOAA datasets and using MSAs within consistent 2010 boundaries, excluding protected/wetlands. Settlement layers are analyzed for expansion/densification trends using zonal statistics in 5-year increments, employing panel regression from 1900-2015 with census division fixed effects to evaluate building patterns affected by SLR and hurricanes. Metrics include structure density, expansion, densification, and MSAs' exposure to SLR.
Highlights: SLR zones have developed faster and have higher structure density than other areas; historically less-developed coastal areas are converging on early-settled SLR zones, many of which are reaching building saturation; coastal conditions, especially hurricane activity, are linked to long-term urban spatial development; developed area and housing stock dramatically increased along the coastline, particularly between 1950 and 2000; Pacific U.S. growth rates converged to those of the Northeast after 1950; coastlines in Florida and the South Atlantic have high SLR exposure; the Western Gulf coastline is a potential hotspot for new building; absolute SLR exposure increased across all divisions; hurricane-affected MSAs are denser and more developed, and hurricane activity is correlated with building patterns; coastal regions are trending towards densification; areas at risk of hurricane impacts are more highly developed; building age is an indicator of vulnerability; coastal communities have denser built environments, leading to high exposure; 'business as usual' coastal development should be avoided.
More Notes: Analysis of U.S. coastal development patterns (expansion and structure density) using HISDAC-US and ZTRAX data reveals faster development and higher structure density in SLR zones, especially in hurricane-prone areas; densification is increasing, suggesting adaptation importance, but saturation and aging infrastructure create vulnerabilities; building size/density increases after hurricanes, compounding hazard exposure; the study focuses on MSAs and advocates for adaptation policies.
Adaptation considered: The paper doesn't explicitly address adaptation strategies or policies. Instead, it focuses on quantifying historical patterns of building development in areas vulnerable to SLR and hurricanes. It analyzes expansion and densification trends, building age, and exposure to SLR, without directly evaluating adaptive responses.

Paper Title: Mortality attributable to PM2.5 from wildland fires in California from 2008 to 2018
Outcome(s) of Interest: Premature deaths, economic impact, mortality burden, PM2.5 exposure, wildland fire, California, long-term outcomes
Drivers of Risk: Wildland fire PM2.5; wildfire smoke exposure; climate change; wildland-urban interface; wildfire management practices.
Methodology: The study estimates premature mortality from wildfire-related PM2.5 in California from 2008-2018 by using Community Multiscale Air Quality modeling at a 12-km grid scale for fire-only PM2.5 concentrations, ZIP code-level mortality data, and a wildfire-specific chronic dose-response coefficient derived from long-term effects of undifferentiated PM2.5 and acute wildfire studies. The approach includes capping PM2.5 concentrations at the 99.9th percentile, applying a Monte Carlo distribution to refine the dose-response value, and using the EPA's VSL for economic valuation.
Highlights: Wildfire PM2.5 in California from 2008-2018 is associated with substantial mortality (52,480 to 55,710 premature deaths) and economic burden ($432 to $456 billion), greater than previous estimates due to the use of a wildfire-specific dose-response coefficient accounting for increased smoke toxicity and CMAQ modeling; mortality impacts are widespread, particularly in population centers; wildfire management is increasingly important due to climate change aridification.
More Notes: The study quantifies premature mortality attributable to wildfire PM2.5 in California from 2008-2018 using modeled data, acknowledging limitations of the model including potential PM2.5 overprediction and lack of isolation of wildfire emissions, and uses existing dose-response values due to the absence of wildfire-specific estimates.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this study. The focus is on estimating premature mortality and economic valuation associated with wildfire PM2.5 exposure using modeling and dose-response functions. The study analyzes the effect of capping high PM2.5 concentrations, which can be interpreted as a mitigation strategy, but doesn't address adaptive measures taken by individuals or communities to reduce exposure or vulnerability.

Paper Title: The potential for coral reef restoration to mitigate coastal flooding as sea levels rise
Outcome(s) of Interest: Mitigation of coastal flooding; reef erosion reversal; reef keeping pace with ~0.5m sea-level rise by 2100 (moderate emissions); persistence of coral-reef ecosystem services.
Drivers of Risk: Sea-level rise, storms (frequency/intensity), coral reef degradation (erosion, disease, mortality), bioerosion, CO2 emissions scenarios.
Methodology: The study combines coral-growth measurements, carbonate-budget assessments (using ReefBudget v1, 2016 data), and hydrodynamic modeling (2D XBeach surfbeat model, validated against a 2016 event) to quantify coastal flooding impacts under various coral-restoration, sea-level rise, and storm scenarios at Buck Island Reef, U.S. Virgin Islands (BIRNM), extending projections to 2100; A. palmata population changes are modeled based on growth, mortality, and fragmentation rates to project potential cover changes; carbonate-budget models project reef elevation change and accretion potential, incorporating tides, waves, and storm surge, also estimating the omission of non-biological erosion following Toth et al. (2022); Statistical analyses were conducted in R Studio using linear mixed-effects models; USGS lidar bathymetry is modified using benthic habitat maps.
Highlights: Coral restoration, particularly of Acropora palmata, can mitigate coastal flooding by reversing reef erosion and enabling reefs to keep pace with ~0.5m of sea-level rise by 2100 under moderate carbon emissions reductions. Sea-level rise is the primary driver of flooding, but reef erosion exacerbates the impact, especially in areas like the northern sector of the reef. Restoration can improve reef accretion, potentially negating the wave-driven component of low-end sea-level-rise scenarios and substantially reducing water levels under higher scenarios, with a large-scale outplanting effort before 2030 showing benefits for 20 years. While sea-level rise is dominant, reef erosion amplifies risk, and restoration can result in sustained increases in coral cover and reef accretion. Hybrid approaches combining coral restoration with engineered structures may be needed under pessimistic scenarios, balancing high costs with direct local value and flood protection. Restoration minimizes climate change impacts by increasing reef elevation and protecting reef structure, whereas inaction amplifies impacts. Carbonate budget models used likely provide a maximum estimate of reef-accretion potential.
More Notes: Buck Island Reef (U.S. Virgin Islands) is the study site, focusing on the shallow reef-crest zone and the impact of Acropora palmata loss on reef-accretion potential and coastal protection. The study uses hydrodynamic modeling and field data to quantify future coastal flooding under various scenarios, acknowledging variability in coral growth, the role of different wave components, and potential for optimistic estimates of reef accretion due to omission of physical erosion and recent coral declines, as well as limitations of methodology and data. Restoration of A. palmata could mitigate coastal flooding by allowing reefs to keep pace with sea-level rise.
Adaptation considered: Coral restoration is modeled to quantify its impact on coastal flooding under sea-level rise and storm scenarios. Reef accretion potential is projected using carbonate-budget and coral-growth models, considering A. palmata cover and reef elevation, under different sea-level-rise scenarios. Restoration scenarios are used to estimate the impact on reef accretion.

Paper Title: Future redistribution of fishery resources suggests biological and economic trade-offs according to the severity of the emission scenario
Outcome(s) of Interest: Future species abundance, distribution changes, Pacific cod, snow crab, fishing yield decline, economic impacts, marine fisheries productivity, SSP scenarios, Dutch Harbor, eastern Bering and Chukchi seas.
Drivers of Risk: Warming, Sea Ice Loss, Primary Production, Species Redistribution, Socioeconomic Factors, Future Climate Scenarios, Fishery Catches, Maximum Catch Potential, SSPs
Methodology: Bioeconomic model analysis projects species-specific abundance changes in the Pacific Arctic using CMIP6 climate projections (SSP126, SSP245, SSP370, SSP585). Present-day climate data (2000-2019) from online repositories is used. Future climate layers (2021-2100) are calculated from down-scaled CMIP6 GCMs using the delta method. Species abundance data, combined with environmental variables (depth, bottom temperatures, summer net primary production), are used to develop random forest (RF) and boosted regression trees (BRT) models to predict present and future species abundance (2061-2080, 2081-2100). Species-specific population growth rates and ensemble model abundance estimates compute Maximum Catch Potential (MCP). Economic impacts are assessed by maintaining constant prices and costs, applicable from 2000-2019 to 2021-2100. Species distribution models project future abundance, focusing on the Bering Sea. Present-day fishing grounds are identified using aggregated fishing effort data. Taxon-specific Centers of Gravity (COGs) are modeled. The analysis projects future MCP under different SSPs relative to the present (2000-2019) for the periods 2041-2060 and 2081-2100, considering distances from major landing ports, and applies bioeconomic analyses to spatio-temporal projections of species abundance, considering SSP126 and SSP585, with buffers around ports. The economic analysis uses ex-vessel prices and fishing costs based on published regional values, assuming constant economic parameters.
Highlights: Climate change impacts on Bering and Chukchi Sea fisheries reveal contrasting species abundance and distribution changes across climate scenarios, with potential winners and losers. SSP126 shows the least change, while SSP585 projects increased Pacific cod but decreased snow crab. Fishing at current distances from Dutch Harbor could yield declining catches of valuable species under SSP585. Redistribution of fishery resources will lead to biological and economic trade-offs, with potential declines in maximum catch potential (MCP), revenue potential (MRP), and profit potential (MPP), particularly in Russian waters. Increased MCP in the NEBS and Chukchi Sea under extreme warming doesn't guarantee increased MRP and MPP, as catch composition shifts impact economic benefits. Shifting resources between US and Russian EEZs raise governance concerns and risks of fisheries conflicts, impacting species like walleye pollock and Pacific cod. Well-managed ecosystems can experience limited near-term economic impact under low to moderate climate changes, but mitigation and climate-smart solutions are crucial under more extreme conditions.
More Notes: Study projects abundance changes for 8 Bering/Chukchi Sea fisheries (2021-2060) under SSP126 and SSP585, assuming constant biological parameters and conservative management, noting potential for increased maximum catch/revenue/profit with scenario severity, shifting species distributions impacting fleet mobility, and acknowledging uncertainties in biological/economic parameters and Earth System Model projections, with focus on the US-Russia EEZ boundary and changes in high-value species like snow crab and Greenland halibut, and consideration of Northern and Southeastern Bering Sea regions.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through scenario-based projections to assess the availability of commercial fisheries under climate change, using projections from machine learning models. These projections help devise climate-ready adaptation and conservation measures for fisheries management in the Eastern Bering Sea and Chukchi Sea. The study accommodates future changes by including distances from major landing ports and fishing grounds in its analyses, which can inform future adaptation strategies.

Paper Title: Which Investors Matter for Equity Valuations and Expected Returns?
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate-induced shifts in asset demand: impact on equity prices and wealth distribution.
Drivers of Risk: Regulatory risk, stakeholder risk (changing preferences), physical risks (long-term), carbon tax policies, investor demand for green firms, environmental scores.
Methodology: An asset demand system is developed, building on Koijen and Yogo (2019), to quantify the impact of investor behavior on asset prices using holdings data from FactSet and firm-level fundamentals from CRSP and Compustat (2000-2019, with a 2010-2019 subsample for environmental/governance data from Sustainalytics). The methodology involves estimating investor-specific demand curves using an instrumental variables shrinkage estimator with a ridge penalty, modeling investor preferences and firm behavior, and incorporating market clearing conditions. Counterfactual prices and valuation ratios are computed by re-estimating cross-sectional valuation regressions with firm characteristics standardized cross-sectionally by quarter and year fixed effects. The model uses a decomposition of the market-to-book ratio and analyzes the relationship between investor characteristics and demand elasticity. Regressions are used to assess the impact of various factors, including environmental scores, on company valuations, and to map changes in valuations to expected returns using models from Cohen, Polk, and Vuolteenaho (2003) and Campbell, Polk, and Vuolteenaho (2009). An expanding window approach is used to compute investment universes, and active share is decomposed into components related to latent demand, portfolio tilts, and elastic demand.
Highlights: Climate-induced shifts in asset demand can significantly impact equity prices and wealth distribution, benefiting long-term investors, banks, and passive investment advisors while potentially causing capital losses for active investment advisors and hedge funds. Foreign investors help lower the cost of capital for green firms in the US and are particularly important for their valuation, while small-active investment advisors are important for the valuation of well-governed firms. Larger, passive, and foreign investors show a stronger demand for greener firms. Small-active investment advisors and hedge funds have the largest impact on prices and are the most influential on pricing of the environmental index; foreign investors influence it the least. A broad shift in investor preferences (stakeholder risk) has a larger impact on firm valuations than regulatory risk. Environmental scores are positively correlated with the market-to-book ratio.
More Notes: The document examines investor behavior, asset pricing, and financial market dynamics, with indirect references to climate risk pricing, highlighting unexplained investor heterogeneity and foreign investor influences on capital costs for greener firms; includes environmental scores from Sustainalytics without directly addressing direct climate impacts relevant to the U.S. National Climate Assessment.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation considerations relate to the integration of climate-related risks into investor behavior and asset pricing frameworks, examining the impact on firm valuations. Environmental and governance factors are assessed using Sustainalytics ratings, highlighting regulatory and stakeholder risks in counterfactual scenarios. Key trends in asset management and their influence on firm valuations and investor welfare are evaluated through a comprehensive framework that captures observable risks and supports climate stress testing. The analysis uses financial data and econometric methods to observe how climate-related scores affect company valuations.

Paper Title: Policy and market forces delay real estate price declines on the US coast
Outcome(s) of Interest: Coastal property values; demographic transitions; coastal gentrification; market adjustments to climate risks.
Drivers of Risk: Sea-level rise (SLR), storms, flooding, beach erosion, reduction in beach nourishment subsidies, shoreline retreat.
Methodology: The Coastal Home Ownership Model (C-HOM), a coupled human-natural system model (agent-based model), simulates long-term coastal real estate market dynamics, incorporating physical coastal attributes, economic values, storm/flood risks, and SLR impacts; it uses a first-stage hedonic price function, user cost framework with a risk premium, and agent-specific parameters with heterogeneous climate beliefs; the model, coded in Matlab R2022b, simulates homeowner/investor decisions (e.g., beach nourishment) considering taxes, property values, and outside markets, with spatial resolution between a town and census block group.
Highlights: Subsidies for coastal management and tax advantages for high-income property owners temporarily mask the effects of sea-level rise (SLR) on coastal property values, delaying price declines and demographic shifts, but ultimately failing to promote long-term adaptation; removing these subsidies would allow prices to more accurately reflect SLR risks, potentially leading to coastal gentrification as high-income owners enter the market, while declining outside markets exacerbate property value declines and demographic shifts, potentially leading to lower-income renters and investor ownership.
More Notes: The C-HOM model explores rising coastal property values and gentrification driven by subsidies and tax advantages in US coastal regions despite SLR and climate risks, using extensive sales data. With fixed housing supply, the model considers local adaptation measures, risk preferences, property fluctuations, and policy tradeoffs, highlighting feedbacks between human and natural systems and examining buyouts with rentbacks.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Beach nourishment (a coastal management approach) is explicitly modeled as an adaptation strategy. Agent-based modeling captures decision-making (voting) on beach nourishment plans based on cost-benefit analysis. The model also explores the effects of subsidies for coastal management on property values.

Paper Title: The short-run, dynamic employment effects of natural disasters: New insights from Puerto Rico
Outcome(s) of Interest: Short-run, dynamic employment effects; overall employment fall; heterogeneous industry responses.
Drivers of Risk: Hurricanes, maximum wind speed, county-level exposure, storm index, NOAA satellite data, thresholds (33 knots, 64 knots), Saffir-Simpson scale (TS, 1-5).
Methodology: The study uses panel local projections to analyze the dynamic employment effects of hurricanes in Puerto Rico from 1995-2019, employing monthly data for 70 3-digit NAICS industries and 78 counties, constructing a hurricane exposure index based on windspeed and population, and exploiting county-time, county-industry-time, and industry-time variation with controls for pre-trends and seasonal effects, while addressing missing data with linear interpolation and performing robustness checks with alternative windspeed measures, geographical centroids, and industry-specific exposure measures.
Highlights: Hurricanes lead to substantial heterogeneity in employment responses across industries in Puerto Rico, with construction typically experiencing employment increases while industries such as accommodation and sightseeing transportation tend to see decreases; this variation is partly due to industry-specific input-output linkages and dynamic effects peak six months post-event, suggesting a construction boom can adversely affect retail, transport, and accommodation sectors.
More Notes: The study examines short-run, dynamic employment effects of hurricanes in Puerto Rico (1995-2019) using monthly data for 70 3-digit NAICS industries and 78 'Municipios' (potentially equivalent to counties), focusing on wind speed as a measure of exposure; it models the force of wind with cubic powers and normalizes windspeed from 0 to 1, defines a tropical storm at 33 knots, controls for past values and fixed effects, considers heterogeneous industry responses and input-output linkages, discusses 'adaptability-driven employment resilience', worker reallocation, and skill transferability between industries (e.g., transport), explores dynamic effects across time horizons (up to 12 months), uses linear interpolation in some specifications, and provides industry employment averages (1995-2017) with 95% confidence bands.
Adaptation considered: The study primarily focuses on quantifying the economic (employment) impacts of hurricanes using panel data analysis and local projections. Adaptation is not explicitly modeled or considered. Instead, the research identifies industries that are 'strengthened', 'weakened', or 'neutral' by hurricanes, which could inform future adaptation strategies. Robustness checks include using different measures of exposure (windspeed thresholds, population weighting, etc.) and controlling for past hurricanes.

Paper Title: Crop rotational diversity can mitigate climate-induced grain yield losses
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced yield loss; Compensation for warm conditions, dry spells, wet/dry conditions; Yield increase with functional richness; Optimal species diversity (2-4).
Drivers of Risk: Temperature anomalies, precipitation anomalies, dry spells (length & temperature), climate change, climate extremes (droughts).
Methodology: Long-term (10-63 years) field experiments across Europe and North America were analyzed, using mixed-effect models in R, to quantify the effects of species diversity (D) and functional richness (FR) in crop rotations (CRD) on small grain and maize yield anomalies under varying climatic conditions (temperature, precipitation, consecutive dry days). The models, based on Equations (2) & (3), predict yield differences from baseline (monoculture under average conditions) at year 15, considering species diversity, climatic anomalies, and functional richness, with outlier handling, multicollinearity checks, and robustness testing. Rotations with species diversity 4-6 were analyzed, with baseline set to yield anomaly of rotation with functional richness 1 under long-term average of CDD and TDD.
Highlights: Crop rotation diversity (CRD) can mitigate climate-induced yield losses in grains and maize, with functionally rich rotations compensating for adverse conditions like warm temperatures, dry spells, and precipitation anomalies; adding even a single species or functional group can counteract losses from substantial climatic changes, offering benefits comparable to improved climatic conditions, though yield benefits peak at specific diversity levels depending on crop and climate, and high-yielding conditions result in higher benefits from species diversity, while low-yielding conditions reduce these benefits; increasing CRD enhances yields over time and improves soil biological health.
More Notes: Long-term studies demonstrate crop rotational diversity (CRD) benefits, including enhanced soil structure, increased water availability, and heat stress mitigation; the impact of CRD on small grain yields depends on crop sequence and species choice, with benefits peaking at two to four species.
Adaptation considered: The paper considers adaptation by analyzing crop yield responses to climate variables and species diversity using long-term experiments and mixed-effects models. Different climate scenarios and species diversity levels are modeled to assess impacts on agricultural outcomes. This approach evaluates how diversification in crop rotations can counteract yield anomalies, thereby serving as a potential adaptation strategy in agricultural practices.

Paper Title: The economic commitment of climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: 19% global income reduction by 2050 (likely range 11-29%), committed damages outweigh 2°C mitigation costs sixfold, committed losses projected for most regions (largest at lower latitudes).
