Drivers of Risk: Average temperature changes; Precipitation changes (total, wet days, extremes); Daily temperature variability; Emission scenarios (RCP2.6, RCP8.5); Climate shocks.
Methodology: The study uses fixed-effects panel regression models with climate and income data from over 1,600 regions (1979-2019) using the W5E5 and DOSE databases, combined with CMIP-6 climate model projections (RCP8.5 and RCP2.6, SSP2 scenario) and mitigation costs from IPCC AR6 IAMs; the analysis employs distributed lag models with first-differenced climate variables, spatial-lag models, block-bootstrapped regressions (1,000 resamples), Monte Carlo simulations to estimate uncertainty arising from climate models, empirical model selection (8-10 lags for temperature, 4 for precipitation), and parameter estimates to project sub-national damages, focusing on committed damages until mid-century (around 2049) and capping moderating variables at the 95th percentile of historical distributions while also employing Bayesian and Akaike information criteria to prevent overfitting.
Highlights: Climate-related economic damages outweigh mitigation costs by sixfold within the next 26 years; a 19% global income reduction is already committed by 2049 (range 11-29%), with North America/Europe experiencing ~11% reductions and South Asia/Africa ~22%; damages diverge strongly across emission scenarios after 2049, highlighting mitigation benefits; temperature changes are primary damage cause, models may underestimate temperature variability and extreme precipitation; spatial spillovers amplify impacts; the study uses lagged climate variables to explore impacts on economic growth, finds precipitation impacts optimal at ~4 lags, temperature at 8-10; provides a robust lower bound on persistence of climate impacts; committed damages are larger for regions with lower GDP and cumulative emissions; several mechanisms support climate variables' impacts on growth, except 'number of wet days' where evidence is lacking; predicts significantly higher economic damages (61.6%) by 2100 under RCP8.5 compared to other studies.
More Notes: The study uses a conservative approach, assuming level effects and avoiding infinite persistence; compares damages to mitigation costs, not a full cost-benefit analysis; presents results globally and regionally; acknowledges conservative damage estimates excluding channels and spillovers; spatial distribution reflects climatic changes and economic vulnerability; uses several climate variables in its models and compares itself to other panel studies; and makes data and code available online.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation considerations in the paper seem limited, primarily focusing on understanding economic impacts under various climate scenarios without explicitly modeling or evaluating adaptation strategies. The analysis centers on empirical impact models and economic growth effects associated with climate variables over time. While the techniques employed (e.g., fixed-effects distributed lag models, Monte Carlo simulations) account for variability and uncertainty, there is no clear mention of incorporating adaptive measures or strategies directly into the models themselves. The study aims to provide projections and estimates on the economic consequences of climate change, with an emphasis on how these are affected by baseline socio-economic and climate projections, rather than how adaptation could mitigate these impacts.

Paper Title: The economic commitment of climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Economic damages; income reduction; regional heterogeneity; distributional impacts.
Drivers of Risk: Average temperature, temperature variability, precipitation (total, wet days, extremes), emission scenarios (RCP2.6, RCP8.5), greenhouse forcing.
Methodology: The methodology uses fixed-effects panel regression models with climate and economic data from over 1,600 regions (1979-2019), incorporating distributed lags (8-10 for temperature, 4 for precipitation), first-differenced climate variables, and CMIP-6 climate projections (RCP8.5, RCP2.6, SSP2). Uncertainty is estimated via Monte Carlo simulations and block-bootstrapped regressions (1,000 resamples), accounting for climate model, empirical model, and lag selection uncertainty. The study employs three empirical specifications focusing on damages committed by mid-century (around 2049) with capped moderating variables, assessing spatial spillovers using a spatial-lag model, while limiting out-of-sample extrapolation; Mitigation costs are derived from integrated assessment models (IAMs) in the IPCC AR6 database.
Highlights: Climate-related economic damages outweigh mitigation costs sixfold within 26 years, committing the world to a 19% income reduction by 2049 (11-29% range) regardless of future emissions. North America and Europe face ~11% income reductions, while South Asia and Africa face ~22%. Damages increase and become more heterogeneous when accounting for multiple climatic components. Climate models may underestimate changes in temperature variability and extreme precipitation. Damages diverge strongly across emission scenarios after 2049, emphasizing the benefits of mitigation. Future changes in average temperature will cause the most damage due to being unprecedented relative to historical fluctuations. Largest committed losses are projected at lower latitudes in regions with lower historical emissions and income. Spatial spillovers can amplify damage magnitude and heterogeneity.
More Notes: The study uses a conservative approach, focusing on level effects and avoiding infinite persistence assumptions; compares damages to mitigation costs without formal cost-benefit analysis; presents results globally and for ten regions; finds spatial distribution of damages reflects complex interactions between climate change and economic vulnerability; acknowledges conservative damage estimates due to excluded channels, spatial spillovers, and adaptation opportunities; makes data and code available; compares empirical specifications and includes mitigation costs; defines climate variables like annual mean temperature, temperature variability, precipitation, wet days, and extreme precipitation; follows IPCC likelihood classification; justifies the use of wet days based on empirical evidence; defines formulas for climate variables; and compares its approach to other panel-based empirical studies.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is largely assumed away (ceteris paribus). The study focuses on exogenous climate impacts relative to socio-economic baselines. It caps moderating variables at the 95th percentile of historical distributions, limiting out-of-sample extrapolation, which may implicitly limit some adaptation. Mitigation costs, but not adaptation, are drawn from IAMs.

Paper Title: Hydroponics: Exploring innovative sustainable technologies and applications across crop production, with Emphasis on potato mini-tuber cultivation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased potato yield, resource efficiency (water, energy, space), reduced land dependency, potential for climate resilience, improved food security.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change impacting land suitability for potato production; drought; unpredictable weather; contaminated water sources; undernutrition in crops; traditional potato seed production limitations (time, cost, disease); nutrient imbalances (potassium); hot/dry locations; equipment failures; power outages; algal growth; clogging (drip systems); drought stress; salinity stress; temperature extremes; challenging environments (deserts, cold terrains, coastal regions); failures in nutrient distribution/water pumps/nozzle clogging; electrical conductivity of nutrient solution.
Methodology: The analysis examines various hydroponic techniques for potato mini-tuber production, including wick systems (with different wicks/media like Jeju scoria, cocopeat, perlite, peat moss), drip systems, aeroponics, deep-water culture, and Nutrient Film Technique (NFT). Studies compare these methods based on water usage, yield productivity, mineral composition, and plant development, also considering cultivars, harvest strategies, growing periods and automation by sensors.
Highlights: Hydroponics, a soilless cultivation system, offers a climate-resilient alternative for potato production, addressing food security concerns by overcoming limitations of soil-based cultivation, providing disease-free mini tubers, and potentially increasing yields. Different hydroponic methods (wick, drip, ebb and flow, deep water culture, NFT, aeroponics) have varying advantages in water conservation, nutrient recirculation, oxygen supply, land usage, and tuberization. Aeroponics, in particular, can significantly increase mini-tuber production compared to conventional systems and is suitable for challenging environments; deep water culture produces small tubers; and NFT systems perform well in terms of tuber number. Optimizing factors like wick configuration, substrate composition, plant density, and real-time monitoring are crucial for success. Nanoparticles can enhance plant tolerance to environmental stresses, and beneficial microorganisms can improve hydroponic system health.
More Notes: Hydroponic systems, including wick, drip, and aeroponic types, are discussed with a focus on potato cultivation and nutrient management (especially potassium). Wick systems are simple but limited, while drip systems can be modernized with data and IoT. Aeroponics has improved, but hydroponics technology is still developing and requires optimized environments and techniques. AI and sensors offer potential for optimization and climate change adaptation. Cultivar selection impacts tuber mass. Asbestos and polyethylene film are used in some hydroponic systems. The authors declare no competing interests.
Adaptation considered: The paper explores hydroponics and soilless agriculture as alternatives to traditional methods for potato mini-tuber production. Different hydroponic techniques (wick, drip, aeroponic, NFT, deep-water culture) and growing media are compared for water usage, yield, mineral composition, and plant development. Customization of misting cycles in aeroponics is noted. Results are cultivar and condition-specific.

Paper Title: Long-term croplands water productivity in response to management and climate in the Western US Corn Belt
Outcome(s) of Interest: Interannual variability in yield, evapotranspiration, and water productivity; impact of irrigation, water input, vapor pressure deficit, and soil water content.
Drivers of Risk: Vapor pressure deficit (VPD), soil water content (SWC), evaporative demand (ET, TA, VPD), water input (WI), air temperature (TA), photosynthetic photon flux density (PPFD), irrigation, rainfed conditions.
Methodology: Long-term (2001-2020) field-scale data from three Eastern Nebraska cropping systems (irrigated continuous maize, irrigated/rainfed maize-soybean rotation) were analyzed using Eddy Covariance (EC) techniques to measure evapotranspiration (ET). ANOVA, TukeyHSD, T-tests, Pearson/Spearman correlations, and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) were employed to assess the interannual variability of yield, ET, water productivity (WP), and their relationships with management practices and climate variables, tested at a 95% confidence interval, with climate data sourced from AmeriFlux.
Highlights: Irrigated sites exhibit significantly higher water productivity (WP) than rainfed sites due to improved yields from enhanced water and nutrient availability; however, rainfed sites may utilize water more efficiently under limited conditions. Soil water content crucially influences soybean yield and WP. Empirical data from 2001 to 2020 show irrigated crops generally outperform rainfed ones in yield, evapotranspiration (ET), and WP, irrespective of crop rotation. Climate variability and management greatly affect maize WP, with yield being the primary driver of interannual WP variability. Effective irrigation scheduling and managing vapor pressure deficit are key to optimizing WP without necessarily relying on increased fertilizer use.
More Notes: Methodological limitations include a lack of replicates within years due to cost, and crop rotation schedules for the three sites (US-Ne1, US-Ne2, US-Ne3) are provided in Figure 2, which uses GPP, NEE, and Re data from the Ameriflux dataset at an hourly time scale.
Adaptation considered: Management practices are considered as factors influencing water productivity (WP) alongside climate variability. Analysis isolates management effects from climate impacts on WP, yield, and evapotranspiration across different cropping systems (irrigated continuous maize, irrigated maize-soybean rotation, and rainfed maize-soybean rotation).

Paper Title: Impact of heat stress on poultry production: Feed intake, water homeostasis, gut integrity, and meat quality
Outcome(s) of Interest: Disrupted production, water homeostasis, gut integrity, meat quality; economic losses, welfare concerns.
Drivers of Risk: Heat stress (HS); Climate change (rising temperatures, frequency of events); High environmental temperatures.
Methodology: Consolidates current research on heat stress effects on poultry, discusses observed effects on broilers (gene expression, gut permeability, intestinal morphology), and references studies using chronic and cyclic heat stress models with dietary interventions.
Highlights: Heat stress adversely affects poultry by reducing feed intake, disrupting water homeostasis, and compromising gut integrity, resulting in decreased growth, reproductive efficiency, and meat quality. It increases water loss, causes respiratory alkalosis, affects gut barrier function, increases gut permeability, and impairs nutrient absorption, leading to pale, soft, exudative meat with reduced water-holding capacity and increased drip loss. Innovative mitigation strategies like sprinkler cooling systems, nutritional additives, and dietary supplements like betaine and resveratrol are critical for maintaining gut integrity, enhancing muscle metabolism, and improving the antioxidant status of broilers.
More Notes: Mitigation strategies for poultry heat stress include nutritional interventions (electrolytes, betaine, vitamin C, probiotics, glutamine), environmental modifications (improved housing, ventilation, cooling), genetic selection for heat tolerance, and precision farming technologies.
Adaptation considered: Dietary supplements are being evaluated as potential interventions for heat stress in poultry.

Paper Title: Forage grass growth under future climate change scenarios affects fermentation and ruminant efficiency
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decreased digestibility, decreased methane production linked to drought/flooding.
Drivers of Risk: Increased temperature, elevated CO2, precipitation changes, altered weather systems, drought, flooding, heat shock, abiotic stress, biotic stress.
Methodology: Ten forage grass varieties were grown for 3 months in growth chambers, replicated three times, under five climate scenarios (2020 control, 2050 predicted, 2050 + flooding, drought, or heat shock); subsequent analyses included chemical analysis (VFA, ammonia, FTIR), gas chromatography, in vitro rumen fermentation (48 hours), VOC analysis, and statistical modeling (ANOVA, general linear models, multivariate linear model, Random Forest, DFA, ls means with Tukey adjustment (P < 0.05)) to assess plant chemistry and rumen fermentation responses.
Highlights: Acute drought and flooding consistently decreased forage digestibility and methane production; elevated temperature and CO2 had minimal impact; stress conditions significantly altered fermentation profiles, volatile organic compound (VOC) profiles, and plant chemical properties, affecting gas production and digestibility; drought and heat stress increased water soluble carbohydrates and protein, while floods reduced them.
More Notes: The study investigates how climate change-induced stress on forage grasses affects rumen fermentation and ruminant feeding strategies, finding that drought and flooding significantly decrease digestibility and methane production, suggesting breeding for stress tolerance is crucial.
Adaptation considered: The study screens different forage grass varieties under various climate scenarios (including drought, flooding, and heat shock) to assess varietal differences in fermentation profiles. This approach evaluates the *potential* for adaptation by identifying grasses that perform better under changing climate conditions.

Paper Title: Efficiency improvement and technology choice for energy and emission reductions of the residential sector
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced global heating demand; increased cooling demand; near-elimination of direct residential emissions; net-zero energy demand potential; heterogeneity of effective strategies.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, temperature changes, local climatic conditions, heating demand, cooling demand, emission prices, regional climate characteristics, extreme weather events, heat island effect, increased floorspace in specific regions.
Methodology: A regional energy system model (TIMER-REMG), part of the IMAGE (IAM) framework, is used for a techno-economic analysis of the global residential sector. The model employs a recursive dynamic approach to explore long-term decarbonization pathways consistent with the Paris Agreement, considering building envelope efficiency, heating/cooling technology choice, and rooftop PV investment. Projections of space heating and cooling demand are based on floorspace, population, heating degree days (HDDs), cooling degree days (CDDs), and cooling intensity, with a multinomial logit function determining rooftop PV adoption. Scenario analysis includes baseline and 2°C scenarios, with varying carbon prices, technology choices, and construction/renovation trends. The model accounts for building-stock turnover, economic decision-making, and the interaction between building envelope and heating/cooling efficiency, projecting energy demand and emissions up to 2100.
Highlights: Residential sector decarbonization is achievable via synergistic strategies combining increased envelope efficiency, advanced heating technologies, and rooftop PV, potentially leading to net-zero energy demand for many households; strategies must account for local climate, socio-economic, and building stock variations.
More Notes: The study models building insulation levels, rooftop PV adoption, and building stock changes across different regions under baseline and 2°C warming scenarios, noting the importance of emission prices, the impact of methodological limitations (data uncertainty, tenant-landlord effect, social dynamics), and the need for grid infrastructure to manage prosumer household electricity variability.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through scenario analysis focusing on building envelope efficiency improvements, technology choices, and the adoption of rooftop PV within residential energy systems. Long-term pathways explore mitigation measures consistent with climate targets. The TIMER-REMG model assesses the impact of efficiency improvements and fuel switching under different scenarios, considering technological advancements and policy impacts, such as carbon pricing, within energy systems. Building-stock turnover and economic decision-making are key components in evaluating adaptation measures.

Paper Title: Long-term croplands water productivity in response to management and climate in the Western US Corn Belt
Outcome(s) of Interest: Significant differences in WP between irrigated and rainfed sites, mainly due to yield variations; water input correlated with rainfed maize WP and yield; vapor pressure deficit negatively correlated with rainfed maize yield and WP; soil water content correlated with soybean yield and WP.
Drivers of Risk: Vapor pressure deficit (VPD), soil water content (SWC), evaporative demand (ET, TA, VPD), water input (WI), photosynthetic photon flux density (PPFD), air temperature (TA), irrigation, climate variability.
Methodology: Long-term data from 2001-2020 were analyzed using Eddy Covariance technique and climate data from AmeriFlux to assess yield, evapotranspiration, and water productivity variability in three cropping systems in Eastern Nebraska. ANOVA, TukeyHSD, T-test, Pearson and Spearman correlations, and PCA were employed to evaluate the effects of management and climate variability, with significance tested at a 95% confidence interval, isolating management effects from climate variability.
Highlights: Water productivity differs significantly between irrigated and rainfed sites, primarily due to yield, with irrigated sites showing higher yields, evapotranspiration, and water productivity due to enhanced water and nutrient availability; soil water content significantly impacts soybean yield, while crop rotation shows no significant effect on yield, evapotranspiration, or water productivity; empirical data from 2001-2020 highlights that yield is a main driver of water productivity variability and optimizing irrigation can enhance yield and water productivity without significantly increasing evaporation or fertilizer-induced transpiration.
More Notes: Methodological details are prominent, including site-specific crop rotations (US-Ne1, US-Ne2, US-Ne3) and use of Ameriflux GPP, NEE, and Re data; however, a lack of within-year replicates is acknowledged as a study limitation.
Adaptation considered: Management practices are considered as potential factors influencing water productivity, alongside climate variability. The analysis aims to isolate management effects from climate effects using long-term data and PCA modeling of different cropping systems.

Paper Title: Forage grass growth under future climate change scenarios affects fermentation and ruminant efficiency
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decreased digestibility, decreased methane production linked to drought/flood.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature increase, elevated CO2, precipitation changes, altered weather systems (drought, flooding, heat shock), abiotic and biotic stresses.
Methodology: Ten forage grass varieties were grown for 3 months in growth chambers under five climate scenarios (current (2020), future (2050), future plus flooding, drought, or heat shock), replicated three times; rumen fermentation was conducted for 48 hours and analyzed using in vitro gas production, FTIR, gas chromatography, and VOC analysis; statistical modeling included ANOVA, general linear models, multivariate linear model, Random Forest, DFA, and ls means with Tukey adjustment (P < 0.05); Random forest classification identified compounds distinguishing climate scenarios.
Highlights: Drought and acute flood conditions significantly decrease forage digestibility and methane production; elevated temperature and CO2 have minimal impact compared to current climate; climate scenarios affect fermentation profiles, volatile organic compound (VOC) profiles, and plant chemical properties; flood reduces total gas production, while drought and heat shock lower methane; stress growth conditions are distinguishable by VOC analysis; VOCs linked to plant stress responses appear during fermentation, suggesting post-ingestion plant metabolism is a factor in the rumen.
More Notes: Forage grass responses to climate change scenarios, including stress memory, were analyzed for effects on rumen degradation, volatile organic compound (VOC) profiles, and in vitro rumen fermentation, indicating that stress treatments like flooding and drought significantly impact digestibility and methane production, suggesting breeding for stress resilience is crucial for maintaining nutritional value.
Adaptation considered: The study screens different forage grass varieties under current and future climate scenarios (including drought, flood, and heat shock). Varietal differences in fermentation profiles are then analyzed, implicitly evaluating the potential for selecting climate-resilient forage.

Paper Title: Efficiency improvement and technology choice for energy and emission reductions of the residential sector
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decarbonization pathways, residential sector, heating demand decrease, cooling demand increase, emissions reduction, energy efficiency, advanced technologies, rooftop PV, net energy demand reduction.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, heating/cooling demand, local climate conditions, regional climate characteristics, emission prices, extreme weather, heat island effect, affluence, building stock, floorspace.
Methodology: The methodology employs the TIMER-REMG model, part of the IMAGE integrated assessment model, to conduct a techno-economic analysis of the global residential sector's pathways under Paris Agreement scenarios, simulating the interplay and adoption of building envelope efficiency, heating/cooling technology, and rooftop PV investments. It models space heating demand based on floorspace, population, and HDD, and cooling demand on population, CDDs, and intensity; a multinomial logit function evaluates the share of electricity met by rooftop PV. The analysis projects energy demand and emissions under Baseline and 2°C scenarios up to 2100, considering carbon pricing and technological advancements, with explicit representation of building-stock turnover, economic decision-making, and construction/renovation trends.
Highlights: Residential sector decarbonization is achievable through a combination of increased envelope efficiency (insulation), advanced heating/cooling technologies (electrification, heat pumps), fuel switching, and rooftop PV, leading to near-zero energy demand for many households. The specific strategies are highly dependent on local climate, socio-economic factors (affluence driving cooling demand), and building stock characteristics. Global heating demand is projected to decrease while cooling demand increases, particularly in Asia, the Middle East & Africa, and Latin America. Insulation improvements are crucial, especially in regions with initially inefficient buildings and expanding floorspace, but technological advancements play a vital role as well. Investments in insulation and efficient technologies are crucial to avoid being locked into inefficient infrastructure. However, wealthier households will disproportionately benefit from efficiency measures and rooftop PV potentially increasing economic inequality.
More Notes: The model incorporates varying insulation levels, dynamic rooftop PV representation, and building stock development to analyze energy efficiency and emissions; improvements are driven by emission prices and vary regionally. Methodological limitations include data requirements, uncertainties regarding household representation, exclusion of tenant-landlord effects and social dynamics of technology adoption, and an assumption of automatic adoption of efficient appliances. Power grid and electricity storage are important for managing prosumer household impacts.
Adaptation considered: Building envelope efficiency improvements, heating/cooling technology choices, rooftop PV investment, and construction/renovation trends for energy efficiency are modeled as adaptation/mitigation measures in response to climate scenarios. Specifically, the interplay between building envelope efficiency and heating/cooling efficiency is considered.

Paper Title: Hydroponics: Exploring innovative sustainable technologies and applications across crop production, with Emphasis on potato mini-tuber cultivation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Resilience to climate change, reduced land dependency, reduced pesticide use, potato yield maintenance/increase, sustainable agriculture, resource efficiency (water, energy, space).
Drivers of Risk: Climatic changes, land suitability loss, drought, unpredictable weather, contaminated water, undernutrition, traditional seed production limitations, nutrient imbalances, equipment failures, power outages, algal growth, clogging, drought stress, salt stress, temperature extremes, challenging environments, failures in nutrient/water distribution, electrical conductivity.
Methodology: The methodology examines various hydroponic systems, including wick, drip, aeroponic, and deep-water cultivation, for potato mini-tuber production, utilizing sensors for environmental monitoring and comparing growing media (e.g., Jeju scoria, cocopeat, perlite, peat moss) and irrigation systems (e.g., NFT, aeroponics, ebb and flow). Studies focus on nutrient delivery methods, optimizing yield and tuber development under different system-specific conditions, with varying cultivars and harvest strategies.
Highlights: Hydroponics is a promising solution for sustainable potato production, addressing food security by overcoming soil limitations, controlling root environment, and enabling year-round pathogen-free mini tuber production in various climates. Specific hydroponic methods like wick systems (optimal horizontal wicks, perlite/peat moss or scoria/peat moss mixes), drip systems (water conservation), ebb and flow (requires monitoring), deep water culture (small tuber production), NFT (high tuber number, nutrient recirculation, climate resilience), and aeroponics (high yields, water efficiency, desert/terrain applications, density management) offer advantages. Nanoparticles can enhance stress tolerance, and beneficial microorganisms can improve plant health. Aeroponics demonstrates significantly higher mini-tuber production compared to conventional and other hydroponic systems. Real-time monitoring is crucial for hydroponic success.
More Notes: Hydroponic systems, including wick (simple indoor, limited by nutrient recycling & oxygen), drip (can be modernized with Big Data/IoT), and aeroponics (initially low yield, improved), are discussed with attention to nutrient management (potassium). Research is needed to optimize yields, environmental control, and techniques; AI and sensors may help adapt to climate change. Asbestos and polyethylene film were components of the hydroponic system tested. Cultivar choice matters (Asterix vs. Agata in aeroponics). The authors declare no conflicts of interest.
Adaptation considered: The paper analyzes hydroponic systems and their potential for crop production (particularly potato mini-tubers) as an alternative to traditional methods. This includes comparing different hydroponic techniques (wick, drip, aeroponic, NFT, deep-water culture), substrates, growing media, irrigation systems, and cultivars. The focus is on optimizing resource use (water, nutrients), yields, and environmental control.
